guest: Panel compilation (Emad Mostaque, Des Traynor, Yann LeCun, Jeff Seibert, Richard Socher, Chris Valenzuela, Tom Tunguz, Miles Grimshaw, Christian Lanng)
guest_role: Heads/founders of Stability AI, Intercom, Meta AI, Digits, You.com, Runway, Theory Ventures, Benchmark, and Beyond Work respectively
assertiveness_why: This is an edited panel-compilation format where Harry mostly acts as a moderator stitching together answers from separate prior interviews, posing the same question to multiple guests to surface disagreement rather than personally challenging any one of them.
question_style: Purely open and topic-routing — Harry poses the same structural question (commoditization, model size, open vs. closed, pricing model, co-pilots, incumbent positioning) to each panelist in turn and lets contradictions between guests do the challenging rather than pushing back himself.
tone: Enthusiastic curator/host energy, explicitly experimental in format, no interruptions or informality since it's a spliced compilation rather than a live conversation.
depth: Very deep and technical across the AI stack: LLM commoditization, parameter-count trends, open vs. closed source economics, infrastructure vs. application value capture, consumption vs. seat-based pricing, co-pilot vs. agent UX paradigms, and company-specific competitive positioning (Apple, Google, Amazon).
harry_quotes: Will we see the commoditization of LLMs?
Are co-pilots an incumbent strategy? How useful are they?
learnings: Panelists split sharply on whether model size still matters: LeCun argued smaller fine-tuned models are increasingly sufficient while Richard Socher argued scale remains essential for multi-task performance.

Tom Tunguz's market-cap analysis found infrastructure (AWS/GCP/Azure) and application-layer public companies have roughly equal aggregate market cap, but far fewer infrastructure winners, implying better odds of venture success at the application layer.
Miles Grimshaw predicts a shift from 'copilot' software (assisting a human seat) to 'control center' software sold against outcomes/SLAs on completed work rather than uptime or per-seat licensing.

Multiple panelists view Google as the most existentially threatened incumbent since its revenue is concentrated in search ads that a conversational AI interface could cannibalize, and it faces an innovator's dilemma in disrupting itself.
LeCun argued AI risk is primarily political/economic (wealth distribution) rather than an inherent technological catastrophe risk, comparing AI skepticism to historical fears about early aviation.
notable: This is an unusual experimental format — a spliced-together 'panel' assembled from clips of separate previously recorded 20VC interviews rather than a single live conversation, explicitly flagged as such by Harry at the open.
